title: Replication to Revolution
concept: A surprising observation becomes a new scientific foundation only after independent teams confirm it with different tools, it generates testable predictions, and those predictions hold when checked.

questions.question: What makes a surprising measurement worth taking seriously?
options: It appears in a peer-reviewed journal
Multiple independent instruments see the same thing
The research team has a strong reputation
questions.explanation: Replication across different instruments rules out equipment errors and random noise—the pattern is real, not an artifact. Publication and reputation don't confirm the signal itself exists.

questions.question: A lab measures an effect no one expected. What's the next threshold before this becomes a breakthrough?
options: Generating predictions that can be tested
Convincing senior scientists in the field
questions.explanation: A real finding must predict something new that wasn't already measured. Predictions test whether you understand the mechanism—without them, you have a data point, not a foundation.

questions.question: Two teams confirm the same surprising result with different methods. Why isn't this sufficient for a paradigm shift?
options: Paradigm shifts require at least five independent confirmations
The predictions generated by the finding must also be validated
Scientific consensus typically takes decades to change
The original theory must be proven completely wrong first
questions.explanation: Replication confirms the signal is real. Validated predictions confirm you understand what's causing it. Both are necessary—a real pattern you don't understand can't replace an existing framework.

questions.question: Why do most surprising experimental results eventually disappear?
options: Journals prefer to publish null results over positive findings
They fail at least one threshold: replication, prediction, or validation
Scientific funding cycles don't support long-term verification
Competing labs suppress findings that threaten their work
questions.explanation: Most anomalies don't survive the three-stage filter. Either other teams can't reproduce them, they don't generate testable predictions, or the predictions fail when checked. The filter works.
